title: Threat model adapter data handling and privacy
description:
## Background
SEAL may inspect project files, plans, evidence, and launch notes. Any app adapter needs clear data boundaries.

## Technical Approach
Document what data is read, stored, transmitted, redacted, and returned for future MCP or App flows.

## Success Criteria
The adapter plan includes privacy and security constraints that can be reviewed before external distribution.

## Test Plan
Use sample workflows to identify sensitive fields and ensure outputs can redact or summarize them.

## Considerations
Codex local workflows and hosted App workflows may have different trust assumptions.

title: Define App-friendly output schemas and UI summaries
description:
## Background
ChatGPT App outputs may need structured cards, reports, or summaries different from raw plugin artifacts.

## Technical Approach
Design output schemas that summarize launch state, blockers, gaps, and evidence links while preserving artifact references.

## Success Criteria
Output schemas can present useful results without exposing unnecessary internal structure.

## Test Plan
Render or inspect mock outputs for beginner readability and traceability.

## Considerations
Keep these schemas derived from artifact state so the App adapter cannot drift from Codex behavior.

title: Create ChatGPT App submission readiness checklist
description:
## Background
Submission requires product, privacy, support, and technical readiness beyond a working local plugin.

## Technical Approach
Create a checklist based on current OpenAI app submission guidance, including auth, data handling, safety, support URLs, testing, and review artifacts.

## Success Criteria
The team can see what remains before attempting App submission and which items are blocked by core plugin work.

## Test Plan
Review checklist against the current docs when this epic becomes active.

## Considerations
This item is intentionally later because the Codex plugin path is the near-term distribution plan.

title: Define stable IDs and cross-artifact reference integrity
description:
## Background
Systems-engineering value depends on traceability. Requirements, components, evidence, risks, and gates need stable IDs and checked references.

## Technical Approach
Specify ID formats, ownership, allowed reference types, and integrity rules across map, spec, trace, proof, risk, and gate files.

## Success Criteria
A reference model document and validator rules prevent dangling IDs, duplicate IDs, and invalid trace link types.

## Test Plan
Create fixtures with duplicate IDs, missing refs, and invalid link types and confirm validation fails with actionable messages.

## Considerations
IDs should be readable enough for humans but stable enough for automation.
close_reason: Defined ID ownership/reference model, added cross-artifact reference validator, updated fixtures, and npm test plus bd graph checks pass.

title: Add artifact versioning and migration policy
description:
## Background
SEAL artifacts will evolve. Existing projects need a safe way to upgrade without losing traceability.

## Technical Approach
Add artifact version metadata, compatibility checks, and a documented migration policy. Implement the first no-op migration path if needed.

## Success Criteria
The validator can detect artifact version mismatches and tell users how to upgrade.

## Test Plan
Run validation on current-version and old-version fixture folders.

## Considerations
Keep migration tooling minimal until schema churn becomes real.
close_reason: Added artifact schema version validation, documented the 0.1.0 migration policy, and covered old/future/malformed schema_version diagnostics through unit, validation, scaffold, and full npm gates.

title: Make artifact schemas authoritative and wired into generation
description:
## Background
The validator and generators need one source of truth for artifact structure. Weak schemas make the rigor claim unverifiable.

## Technical Approach
Define JSON Schema or equivalent structured schemas for each artifact type and ensure generation code writes data that conforms to them.

## Success Criteria
Every generated artifact has an authoritative schema and the generator cannot silently produce invalid structure.

## Test Plan
Run schema validation against passing and failing fixtures.

## Considerations
Use structured parsers and validators rather than ad hoc string checks.
close_reason: Added authoritative artifact schemas, schema registry, generator validation, passing/failing fixture tests, and npm test passes.

title: Create beginner-safe MAP, IMPACT, and PROOF templates
description:
## Background
Non-expert users need generated artifacts that are useful immediately and do not expose empty intimidating structures.

## Technical Approach
Build templates for .seal/map.yaml, .seal/impacts/IMPACT-*.yaml, .seal/proof.yaml, .seal/evidence/index.yaml, and gap/debt records with starter sections, plain-language labels, and examples.

## Success Criteria
The first generated .seal folder is complete, readable, includes MAP/IMPACT/PROOF scaffolding, and is ready for iterative refinement.

## Test Plan
Generate artifacts from a one-page plan and inspect whether each file has meaningful initial content.

## Considerations
Templates should not hide uncertainty. Unknowns should be recorded as assumptions or gaps.
close_reason: Generated MAP/IMPACT/PROOF/evidence templates now include beginner guidance and visible proof gaps; templates test and npm test pass.

title: Define source authority registry and artifact trust states
description:
## Background
SEAL must distinguish human-approved facts, external authoritative sources, repo observations, execution evidence, mathematical or code proof, LLM inference, and unknowns.

## Technical Approach
Define .seal/sources.yaml or equivalent source registry plus shared fields: source_refs, authority_state, approval_state, and confidence. Ensure meaningful artifacts can cite sources and cannot treat inference as approved baseline truth.

## Success Criteria
Every meaningful artifact record can carry source_refs, authority_state, approval_state, and confidence, with allowed states such as human_approved, repo_observed, externally_sourced, mathematically_proven, inferred, and unknown.

## Test Plan
Create fixtures for each source type and verify validation fails when an approved baseline record has only inferred or unknown authority.

## Considerations
Keep drafts flexible. The hard rule is that LLM-inferred records may guide investigation but cannot become baseline truth without source authority.
close_reason: Implemented source authority registry fields and validation; npm test, bd dep cycles, and bd status pass.

title: Add CI smoke suite for plugin and artifacts
description:
## Background
The plugin needs regression protection before public distribution.

## Technical Approach
Configure CI to run linting, schema validation, fixture tests, and any plugin packaging checks that can run headlessly.

## Success Criteria
A fresh pull request shows whether the core SEAL workflow still works.

## Test Plan
Push a branch or run CI locally if supported and confirm expected checks pass.

## Considerations
Keep CI fast. Deep evaluations or manual Codex UI checks can remain outside the headless suite.
close_reason: Added npm run ci:smoke as a fast deterministic local smoke suite covering schema validation, full fixture workflow, plugin manifest, skill routing, launch report generation, plugin smoke, and artifact validation; added GitHub Actions workflow that invokes the same command. Local npm test, npm run ci:smoke, and bd dep cycles passed. Remote PR verification is blocked by seal-publish-remote because git remote -v is empty.

title: Build task-focused context packs for Codex
description:
## Background
SEAL should help Codex work from the right evidence without loading the entire repo or drifting away from approved artifacts.

## Technical Approach
Build compact context packs from .seal/map.yaml, impact records, proof gaps, source authority, and relevant files. Include only the artifacts, file summaries, unknowns, and gates needed for the current task.

## Success Criteria
Given a proposed change, SEAL can produce a small context pack that names the relevant components, files, interfaces, tests, claims, evidence, and unknowns.

## Test Plan
Run context-pack generation on fixture repos and verify it includes expected relevant records while excluding unrelated files.

## Considerations
Context packs should preserve source authority and confidence markers so inferred records do not become baseline truth.
close_reason: Added context-pack builder and CLI preserving source authority across relevant map, impact, proof, evidence, gap, and test records; focused context-pack test, full suite, and bead graph checks pass.

title: Create example workflows for plan, gstack, and existing project inputs
description:
## Background
Examples will show how SEAL complements gstack and helps maintain real projects.

## Technical Approach
Create three small examples: one plain Markdown plan, one gstack-style plan, and one existing project. Show inputs, commands, outputs, and next actions.

## Success Criteria
Examples demonstrate the main value propositions without requiring a large sample repo.

## Test Plan
Run each example through the current plugin workflow and validator.

## Considerations
Examples should expose imperfect plans and show how SEAL handles gaps.
close_reason: Added runnable example workflows for Markdown plan, gstack-style plan, and existing repository inputs, with tests that execute the documented command paths.

title: Write guided first-run for non-expert Codex users
description:
## Background
The first-run guide is the key usability artifact for users who are not systems engineers.

## Technical Approach
Write a short guide that explains what SEAL is, what command or skill prompt to run, what files it creates, what questions it may ask, what done looks like, and what to do when it finds unknowns. Include the prompt: Use SEAL to map this repo and tell me what is unknown.

## Success Criteria
A user can complete the first workflow without knowing SEAL internals or systems-engineering terminology.

## Test Plan
Have the guide executed from a clean clone and update any missing steps.

## Considerations
Keep troubleshooting practical and tied to likely installation or validation errors.
close_reason: Rewrote plugin/docs/first-run.md as a guided non-expert workflow with Codex prompt, terminal commands, generated artifacts, done criteria, unknown handling, troubleshooting, and clean first-run check; added tests/first-run-docs.test.mjs to execute the documented CLI path on a clean fixture and verified with node tests\\first-run-docs.test.mjs, node tests\\glossary.test.mjs, node tests\\scaffold.test.mjs, node tests\\plugin-smoke.test.mjs, npm test, bd dep cycles, and bd status.

title: Epic: SEAL artifact model, schemas, and migrations
description:
## Background
SEAL needs durable artifacts that survive beyond a single agent session and directly support the three product engines: MAP, IMPACT, and PROVE.

## Technical Approach
Make the .seal artifact model explicit, schema-backed, source-authority-aware, and easy for Codex to generate and validate. Center P0 on .seal/map.yaml, .seal/impacts/IMPACT-*.yaml, .seal/proof.yaml, and .seal/evidence/.

## Success Criteria
The artifact set has authoritative schemas, templates, reference integrity rules, source authority, and examples that support ingestion, repo mapping, impact analysis, proof gates, and launch reports.

## Test Plan
Validate generated fixtures and confirm cross-file references are checked.

## Considerations
Avoid schema sprawl. Start with the minimum model needed for traceability, proof, and launch readiness.
close_reason: P0 closeout audit verified schemas, templates, source authority, reference integrity, example artifacts, and validation tests support the artifact model promise.

title: Epic: Later ChatGPT App and MCP adapter
description:
## Background
The user asked to consult ChatGPT App submission guidance, but the primary product is a Codex plugin. ChatGPT App work should be a later adapter, not the core path.

## Technical Approach
Design a clean adapter boundary where the same SEAL workflow can eventually expose MCP tools and App-friendly outputs without distorting the Codex plugin.

## Success Criteria
There is a documented adapter plan, tool contract, privacy review, and submission readiness checklist gated behind a working Codex plugin.

## Test Plan
Validate adapter contracts with mock inputs before building a submitted app.

## Considerations
Do not spend implementation effort here until core plugin, artifacts, validation, and launch reports are usable.

title: Epic: Installable Codex plugin core
description:
## Background
SEAL is intended to be used and advertised primarily as a Codex plugin. The core deliverable is an installable plugin with real skill entrypoints, clear metadata, and local smoke tests.

## Technical Approach
Bring the extracted plugin plan into the workspace, align it with current Codex plugin mechanics, implement the actual invocation surface, and prove it loads locally.

## Success Criteria
A user can install or load the plugin in Codex, invoke the SEAL workflow from a plan or repo, and receive a valid artifact set with beginner-oriented guidance.

## Test Plan
Run a local plugin load smoke, invoke the main workflow on fixtures, and validate generated artifacts.

## Considerations
Do not promise slash commands unless the implemented Codex surface actually supports them. Prefer skill-based entrypoints if that is the reliable plugin mechanism.
close_reason: All P0 plugin core children are complete: scaffold, manifest, skill routing, invocation, context pack, and plugin smoke. npm test passes, generated artifacts validate, and bd dep cycles/status are clean.

title: Epic: Guided plan and project ingestion
description:
## Background
The first useful SEAL experience is converting an existing plan, gstack output, or code project into a rigorous but understandable artifact set.

## Technical Approach
Build ingestion workflows for both new projects and existing repos. Extract goals, constraints, assumptions, requirements, components, risks, proof needs, launch gates, and source-authority gaps from user-provided inputs and repo observation.

## Success Criteria
A non-expert can point SEAL at a plan or repo and receive a usable first-pass systems map with clear gaps and next actions.

## Test Plan
Run ingestion against representative Markdown, gstack-style, and existing-repo fixtures.

## Considerations
The workflow should be transparent about inferred content and should separate user-provided facts from agent assumptions.
close_reason: P0 closeout audit verified Markdown, GStack-style plan, and repository ingestion produce maps, gaps, next actions, and passing validation coverage.

title: Epic: Proof, gates, and launch readiness
description:
## Background
SEAL differentiates itself by turning plans and projects into evidence-backed launch decisions. Proof and gates are the product core after ingestion.

## Technical Approach
Define claim types, evidence records, gate policies, and launch readiness reports that are understandable to beginners but rigorous enough for engineering decisions.

## Success Criteria
A user can see what is proven, what is assumed, what is blocked, and what must happen before launch.

## Test Plan
Run launch readiness on fixtures with passing, failing, and incomplete evidence.

## Considerations
The system should make uncertainty visible. A red or blocked gate is a useful outcome.
close_reason: P0 closeout audit verified proof taxonomy, evidence store, gap reports, gate policy, and launch readiness reports expose proven, assumed, blocked, and pre-launch work.

title: Epic: Release, documentation, and public distribution
description:
## Background
The plugin needs enough packaging, documentation, examples, and release discipline for users to install it and trust it.

## Technical Approach
Create release docs, installation instructions, examples, changelog, packaging checks, and public-facing workflow demos once the core path works.

## Success Criteria
A new user can install SEAL, run the first workflow, inspect generated artifacts, and understand limitations.

## Test Plan
Run a clean install and first workflow from the published docs.

## Considerations
Documentation should be practical and workflow-first, not a systems-engineering textbook.
close_reason: All release distribution children are complete: first-run guide, example workflows, release checklist, and marketplace launch assets. Full npm test, plugin smoke, launch-report smoke, and bd dep cycles passed during the final child bundle.

title: Epic: Repository mapping and change impact analysis
description:
## Background
SEAL should help users maintain and improve projects, not just create initial plans. Repo mapping and impact analysis are the product: users need to see what exists, what depends on what, what breaks when change happens, and what proof is required.

## Technical Approach
Build the P0 golden path around a fully visible repo map, explicit unknowns, rendered views, impact records, and proof obligations. Fully mapped means nothing is invisible: every non-ignored file is classified, mapped to a component or recorded as an unknown gap.

## Success Criteria
Users can ask what a repo contains and what a change affects, then receive linked components, files, interfaces, tests, proof gaps, risks, and gates.

## Test Plan
Run repo mapping and impact analysis on controlled fixture repos and fixture changes with known expected affected artifacts.

## Considerations
Impact analysis should be conservative. Unknown impact is a valid result when evidence is missing, and unknowns must be visible.
close_reason: All P0 repo map and impact children are complete: inventory, component classification, rendered views, unmapped debt, change impact, and proof obligations. npm test passes and bd dep cycles/status are clean.

title: Epic: Non-expert user promise and product shape
description:
## Background
SEAL must be approachable for users who are not systems engineers. The P0 promise is that a user can install the Codex plugin, initialize .seal, ingest a plan or existing repo, map the system, analyze impact, attach proof, and get a launch/readiness report.

## Technical Approach
Define the smallest usable product contract around MAP, IMPACT, and PROVE. Treat gstack usability as the baseline for approachability and SEAL rigor as the differentiator, while keeping persona polish from blocking the engine.

## Success Criteria
The repository has a one-page product contract, beginner vocabulary, and first-run workflow that explain the golden path: initialize, ingest, map, impact, prove, validate, and launch report.

## Test Plan
Review the brief against a gstack-style plan, a hand-written Markdown plan, and an existing code project. Confirm each can be explained without systems-engineering jargon.

## Considerations
Use the safer promises: zero hidden technical debt, no untraced rework, disciplined traceability, explicit assumptions, and evidence-backed launch readiness.
close_reason: P0 closeout audit verified product contract, glossary, personas, first-run guide, invocation surface, and tests cover the promised MAP/IMPACT/PROVE/VALIDATE/LAUNCH workflow.

title: Epic: Validation, fixtures, and regression safety
description:
## Background
The earlier plan review found the validator too weak for the product claim. Validation must prove schemas, references, coverage, and reports are trustworthy.

## Technical Approach
Build a focused validation suite with passing and failing fixtures, CI checks, and useful error messages that non-experts can act on.

## Success Criteria
Invalid artifacts fail loudly with actionable messages, and representative happy paths remain green in CI.

## Test Plan
Run schema, reference, coverage, ingestion, and launch-readiness fixtures locally and in CI.

## Considerations
Validation is both a technical guardrail and a user trust feature.
close_reason: P0 closeout audit verified schema, reference, file coverage, authority, full workflow pass/fail fixtures, actionable validation errors, and plugin smoke tests.

title: Create pass and fail fixtures for the full workflow
description:
## Background
Fixtures are needed for implementation safety, documentation, and user confidence.

## Technical Approach
Create small fixture inputs and expected outputs for the P0 golden path: initialize .seal, ingest a Markdown plan or existing repo, map, render views, analyze impact, create proof obligations, validate, and generate launch readiness.

## Success Criteria
The test suite has compact fixtures that cover MAP, IMPACT, PROVE, launch reporting, and common failure modes.

## Test Plan
Run all fixtures through the validator and snapshot or semantic assertions.

## Considerations
Keep fixtures readable. They will double as examples for contributors and users.
close_reason: Added compact pass and fail full-workflow fixtures covering map, impact, proof, validation, and launch readiness; focused fixture test, full suite, and bead graph checks pass.

title: Define entry and exit criteria for plan, build, prove, and launch
description:
## Background
SEAL needs clear movement rules so Codex and users know what must be true before progressing from plan to build to prove to launch.

## Technical Approach
Define hard-fail and warn criteria for each phase. P0 hard failures include invalid schemas, broken refs, claims with neither evidence nor gap, unmapped files in launch report, and launch artifacts that omit known unknowns.

## Success Criteria
Gate criteria are explicit enough for a validator to enforce and plain enough for a non-expert to understand.

## Test Plan
Evaluate fixtures where each hard-fail and warn condition is triggered exactly once.

## Considerations
P0 gates should avoid over-blocking early exploration while being strict about false launch confidence.
close_reason: Defined structured plan/build/prove/launch gate criteria with hard-fail and warning rules, documented plain-language movement rules, and added isolated trigger fixtures for every criterion; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Implement launch gate policy evaluation
description:
## Background
Launch readiness needs explicit gates rather than vague confidence. Gates should explain pass, fail, blocked, and unknown states.

## Technical Approach
Implement policy evaluation from entry and exit criteria. Consume map coverage, impact records, proof claims, evidence, gaps, source authority, and risks. Fail hard for invalid schemas, broken refs, claims with neither evidence nor gap, unmapped launch files, and hidden unknowns.

## Success Criteria
A gate report clearly states which gates pass, fail, warn, are blocked, or need more evidence, with every decision linked to artifacts.

## Test Plan
Run policy evaluation on passing, failing, blocked, warning, and unknown fixtures.

## Considerations
Gate defaults should be conservative for launch and permissive enough for early discovery.
close_reason: Implemented gate policy evaluation with pass, fail, warn, blocked, and unknown decisions linked to validation, impact, proof, evidence, and launch artifacts; focused policy tests, npm test, and bd graph checks pass.

title: Implement change impact analysis across artifacts
description:
## Background
Users maintaining a project need to know what a proposed change affects before they edit blindly.

## Technical Approach
Given a changed file, requirement, component, interface, invariant, schema, dependency, data store, or gate, traverse trace links and repo mappings to identify affected artifacts and unknowns. Write .seal/impacts/IMPACT-*.yaml.

## Success Criteria
Impact output lists affected requirements, components, files, interfaces, invariants, schemas, tests, services, dependencies, costs, proofs, risks, launch gates, and reasons.

## Test Plan
Create fixture changes and assert expected affected IDs and unknown impact records.

## Considerations
When trace links are incomplete, the output should say what evidence is missing instead of pretending confidence.
close_reason: Implemented seal-impact change scope generation with schema/reference-valid affected records, explicit unknown impact gaps, docs, and npm test plus bd graph checks passing.

title: Generate proof obligations from proposed changes
description:
## Background
The maintenance value of SEAL comes from turning changes into concrete verification work.

## Technical Approach
Map impacted requirements and components to required evidence such as tests, repo execution, review notes, demos, measurements, external references, human approvals, or manual checks.

## Success Criteria
Each impact analysis produces proof_required and approval_needed records that must be satisfied or explicitly gapped before launch gates pass.

## Test Plan
Run on fixture changes that require unit tests, integration tests, docs updates, human approval, and manual validation.

## Considerations
Proof obligations should be actionable and not simply restate that testing is needed.
close_reason: Implemented proof_required and approval_needed impact obligations with schema, reference integrity, docs, and regression coverage; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Implement existing project ingestion from a repo path
description:
## Background
Most users will arrive with an existing project and ask what is going on. Existing-project ingestion is core to the product promise, not a later adapter.

## Technical Approach
Inventory the repository, classify every file, identify likely entrypoints and modules, and create a first-pass map with unknowns, proof gaps, and authority gaps.

## Success Criteria
Pointing SEAL at a repo produces .seal/map.yaml, an unknown/debt register, proof gaps, and a validation plan that distinguish observed facts from inference.

## Test Plan
Use at least one tiny app fixture and one multi-directory fixture, and verify every non-ignored file is classified or recorded as unknown.

## Considerations
Separate static facts from inferred architecture. Never overstate understanding of business requirements recovered from code alone.
close_reason: Repo invocation now writes classified-or-gapped file coverage, repo validation plan, and proof gaps; repo ingestion fixtures and npm test pass.

title: Generate an assumption and gap review after ingestion
description:
## Background
A non-expert user needs help seeing what is missing from a plan or project before launch work begins.

## Technical Approach
After ingestion, produce a concise review of missing requirements, unclear interfaces, unproven claims, unmanaged risks, and launch blockers.

## Success Criteria
Every ingestion run ends with actionable gaps ranked by launch impact and confidence.

## Test Plan
Use fixtures with intentionally missing constraints, unclear users, and absent test evidence.

## Considerations
The review should guide next steps without shaming the user or drowning them in terminology.
close_reason: Generated ranked ingestion gap review after plan/repo ingestion, wired invocation/inventory/CLI output, and covered missing requirements, unclear interfaces, proof gaps, debt, launch blockers, and source authority with regression tests; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Implement gstack-style plan ingestion
description:
## Background
The intended workflow includes plans created with gstack. SEAL should make those plans maintainable and launchable with added rigor.

## Technical Approach
Collect or define representative gstack-like sections and map them into SEAL artifacts. Preserve original plan intent while adding traceability, evidence needs, and launch gates.

## Success Criteria
A gstack-style input can be converted into SEAL artifacts with a clear import report explaining what was mapped, inferred, or left unresolved.

## Test Plan
Run import fixtures and compare the output to expected artifact snapshots.

## Considerations
If exact gstack formats vary, start with a tolerant Markdown importer plus named gstack heuristics.
close_reason: Implemented tolerant gstack-style Markdown ingestion with mapped decisions, milestones, risks, assumptions, launch gates, an explicit gstack import review gap, import report output, fixture coverage, and focused validation.

title: Implement Markdown plan ingestion
description:
## Background
Many users will start with a free-form plan document. SEAL needs to turn that into structured artifacts without requiring a special template.

## Technical Approach
Parse headings, bullets, decisions, constraints, milestones, and acceptance criteria from Markdown. Map extracted content into artifacts with confidence and assumption annotations.

## Success Criteria
A free-form Markdown plan produces initial map, spec, trace, risks, assumptions, and launch gates.

## Test Plan
Use at least three Markdown fixtures: sparse, medium, and detailed.

## Considerations
Do not invent certainty. Low-confidence extraction should become review items.
close_reason: Implemented source-backed Markdown plan ingestion for sparse, medium, and detailed plans with map schema/reference/authority support; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Generate beginner-readable launch readiness report
description:
## Background
The final output must help a non-expert decide what to do next without reading raw artifact files.

## Technical Approach
Generate a concise report with launch status, map coverage, unmapped debt, impact status, proof gaps, top blockers, high-risk assumptions, gate decisions, and next actions. Link every conclusion back to artifacts.

## Success Criteria
The report is understandable on its own, traceable to structured evidence, and honest about unknowns rather than hiding them.

## Test Plan
Compare generated reports for pass, fail, and incomplete fixture states.

## Considerations
Avoid a wall of process language. The report should feel like a practical readiness decision.
close_reason: Implemented traceable launch readiness reporting with pass, fail, and incomplete fixture coverage plus CLI/docs wiring; focused and full tests plus bead graph checks pass.

title: Classify components and interfaces from repo evidence
description:
## Background
A system map needs components, purposes, interfaces, dependencies, data stores, tests, and ownership boundaries that can be traced to files.

## Technical Approach
Use inventory output, package metadata, imports, route definitions, configuration, docs, and source authority to propose components and interfaces with confidence levels. Every non-generated, non-vendored file should have a purpose, owning component, dependencies or none, interfaces touched or none, tests/proof or gap, and authority marker.

## Success Criteria
The system map links components to source files, owners/reasons for dependencies, data stores, tests, and explicit unknowns for anything not confidently mapped.

## Test Plan
Compare classifier output for fixture projects against expected component maps and expected unknown records.

## Considerations
Keep confidence and provenance visible so users can correct the map.
close_reason: Repo maps now infer component ownership, dependencies, interfaces, test links, data-store hints, and explicit proof gaps from repo evidence; repo ingestion and npm test pass.

title: Build repository inventory engine
description:
## Background
SEAL needs a repeatable view of every non-ignored file, language, framework, test location, doc, asset, generated file, vendored file, migration, and likely runtime entrypoint.

## Technical Approach
Implement inventory using filesystem traversal, ignore rules, package metadata, and language-specific lightweight heuristics. Classify each file as product_code, test, config, build, script, documentation, artifact, generated, vendored, asset, migration, or unknown.

## Success Criteria
The inventory writes .seal/map.yaml with 100 percent classification for non-ignored files and marks uncertain files as unknown instead of dropping them.

## Test Plan
Run on fixture repos and verify ignored files, generated files, vendored files, assets, migrations, and test directories are classified correctly.

## Considerations
Avoid expensive full parsing as the first step. Prefer useful coarse structure that can improve over time.
close_reason: Added repository inventory traversal, classification, .seal/map.yaml writer, CLI entrypoint, fixture coverage, and npm test plus bd graph checks pass.

title: Render Markdown and Mermaid system map views
description:
## Background
Non-experts need to see the mapped repo as a visual and written system map, not only raw YAML.

## Technical Approach
Generate Markdown and Mermaid views from .seal/map.yaml showing components, file ownership, dependencies, interfaces, data stores, tests, and unknowns.

## Success Criteria
A repo map run produces a readable Markdown report and Mermaid diagram that match the structured map and visibly include unknowns.

## Test Plan
Render fixture maps and compare stable sections or snapshots for components, dependencies, and unknowns.

## Considerations
Ship Mermaid and Markdown before any custom UI.
close_reason: Implemented Markdown and Mermaid map rendering from .seal/map.yaml, wired repo inventory to emit views, and covered components, dependencies, interfaces, tests, data stores, and unknown gaps with regression tests; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Prepare marketplace and launch assets
description:
## Background
If advertised as a Codex plugin, SEAL needs clear marketplace-style assets and launch copy.

## Technical Approach
Prepare concise description, screenshots or terminal captures, supported inputs, limitations, support link, and example outputs.

## Success Criteria
The launch package explains SEAL in practical terms and routes users to the first-run guide.

## Test Plan
Review assets against actual plugin behavior after smoke tests pass.

## Considerations
Do not create launch assets that imply ChatGPT App availability before that adapter exists.
close_reason: Added marketplace and launch assets documentation with supported inputs, outputs, terminal capture recipes, limitations, support routing, first-run guide routing, manifest registration, and doc/scaffold test coverage. Verified with targeted doc/manifest/scaffold tests, npm test, plugin smoke, launch-report smoke, and bd dep cycles.

title: Design MCP tool contract for SEAL workflows
description:
## Background
A future ChatGPT App or MCP integration needs stable tools that wrap SEAL workflows cleanly.

## Technical Approach
Define candidate tools such as ingest_plan, map_project, validate_artifacts, analyze_impact, and generate_launch_report with inputs, outputs, and error shapes.

## Success Criteria
A tool contract document exists and maps each tool to existing Codex plugin workflow capabilities.

## Test Plan
Review each tool against fixture workflows and confirm no extra app-only logic is required.

## Considerations
Tool contracts should return structured data plus concise user-facing summaries.
close_reason: Completed MCP tool contract documentation, fixture review commands, command mappings, response/error shapes, privacy invariants, and regression coverage in tests/mcp-tool-contract-docs.test.mjs.

title: Implement the real SEAL invocation surface in Codex
description:
## Background
The earlier plan referenced slash commands, but that claim needs to be replaced or implemented using the actual Codex plugin mechanism.

## Technical Approach
Define the canonical invocation path, likely a skill entrypoint, with commands or prompts that accept a plan file, gstack output, or repo path and route to the workflow.

## Success Criteria
The documented invocation works in Codex and does not mention unsupported command surfaces.

## Test Plan
Invoke the plugin locally on a minimal Markdown plan and confirm the expected artifact creation flow starts.

## Considerations
Favor a dependable explicit workflow over clever command aliases. Non-expert users should not need to know internal file names.
close_reason: Added supported skill-based invocation docs, seal-invoke CLI, plan/repo artifact starter, manifest/package wiring, and passing invocation, manifest, npm, and bead graph checks.

title: Align Codex plugin manifest and marketplace metadata
description:
## Background
Plugin distribution depends on metadata that accurately describes entrypoints, permissions, skills, versioning, and intended usage.

## Technical Approach
Review plugin metadata, README install steps, marketplace copy, version fields, and skill references against the current OpenAI plugin and Codex documentation.

## Success Criteria
Plugin metadata is internally consistent, references real files, and clearly positions SEAL as a Codex plugin for plan-to-launch rigor.

## Test Plan
Run any available manifest validation and manually inspect paths referenced by the metadata.

## Considerations
Treat ChatGPT App submission metadata as separate from the Codex plugin manifest unless the architecture explicitly supports both.
close_reason: Added validated Codex plugin manifest, manifest schema, path/package consistency checks, docs updates, and focused plus full npm tests with clean bead graph.

title: Materialize the plugin skeleton in the SEAL workspace
description:
## Background
The current workspace was empty before Beads initialization. The planned plugin files need to be copied or reconstructed into this repo before implementation can proceed.

## Technical Approach
Move the relevant plan archive contents into the workspace with a clean repository layout, preserving only files that belong to the plugin product.

## Success Criteria
The repo has a clear plugin root, skill files, schemas, tests, fixtures, docs, and package metadata with no stray temp extraction paths.

## Test Plan
Run a tree inspection and verify expected top-level directories exist and no generated junk or personal paths are committed.

## Considerations
Keep this scoped to materializing the product skeleton. Do not refactor implementation details before the files are under version control.
close_reason: Materialized plugin root, skill entrypoint, schemas, fixtures, docs, package metadata, and scaffold test; npm test passed.

title: Harden skill routing for beginner and advanced modes
description:
## Background
SEAL should guide non-experts while still allowing advanced users to work directly with artifacts and validators.

## Technical Approach
Create or refine skill instructions that inspect first, infer cautiously, and ask only for authority gaps. Route beginner requests through guided MAP, IMPACT, and PROVE steps, while advanced users can operate directly on artifacts.

## Success Criteria
A non-expert can ask "Use SEAL to map this repo and tell me what is unknown" and receive the golden path without learning requirements-engineering terms.

## Test Plan
Use scripted prompt examples to confirm the correct mode is selected for a vague beginner request, an existing-repo request, and a precise expert request.

## Considerations
Mode selection should reduce friction, not create a long questionnaire before useful work happens.
close_reason: Added tested SEAL request routing for beginner repo, impact, proof, and advanced artifact modes; skill instructions document inspect-first and authority-gap behavior; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Add local Codex plugin load and workflow smoke test
description:
## Background
A plugin intended for Codex must have a repeatable local smoke test that proves installation, loading, invocation, and basic artifact generation.

## Technical Approach
Document and automate the shortest possible local smoke path. It should cover plugin discovery, main entrypoint invocation, fixture input, generated artifact output, and validation.

## Success Criteria
A contributor can run one documented smoke procedure and know whether the plugin is usable in Codex.

## Test Plan
Run the smoke test from a clean clone or clean working tree after plugin files exist.

## Considerations
Record any manual Codex UI steps separately from automated checks so failures are diagnosable.
close_reason: Added documented plugin smoke path plus automated manifest discovery, fixture invocation, artifact generation, and validation checks; verified with node tests\\plugin-smoke.test.mjs, node tests\\plugin-manifest.test.mjs, node tests\\scaffold.test.mjs, npm test, bd dep cycles, and bd status.

title: Create plain-language systems-engineering glossary and UX copy map
description:
## Background
Terms like traceability, gates, hazards, validation, and verification are valuable but can intimidate non-expert users.

## Technical Approach
Build a glossary that maps expert terms to short plain-language labels and question phrasing. Use questions like who is this for, what should never happen, what would prove this worked, and can users lose work here.

## Success Criteria
A glossary file exists and every public-facing workflow uses beginner-safe language while preserving rigorous internal artifact names.

## Test Plan
Sample generated output should include plain labels and optional deeper definitions, not unexplained acronyms or requirements-engineering prompts.

## Considerations
The glossary should become a lint target later, but the first pass can be documentation and examples.
close_reason: Added plugin/docs/glossary.md with beginner-safe labels, starter questions, and UX copy map; exposed it through manifest/docs/skill routing, added route plain labels and validation, and verified with node tests\glossary.test.mjs, node tests\skill-routing.test.mjs, node tests\scaffold.test.mjs, node tests\plugin-manifest.test.mjs, npm test, bd dep cycles, and bd status.

title: Define the gstack bridge and comparison story
description:
## Background
SEAL should be as usable as gstack while helping users maintain, improve, and launch plans with systems-engineering rigor.

## Technical Approach
Document how SEAL compares to gstack, what gstack artifacts it can ingest, and the user story for moving from gstack plan creation into SEAL maintenance and launch readiness.

## Success Criteria
A comparison note exists with positioning, import assumptions, example inputs, and a clear no-attack framing that treats gstack as a plan-generation complement.

## Test Plan
Use one representative gstack-style plan and verify the note identifies how SEAL would convert it into map, spec, trace, proof, and gate artifacts.

## Considerations
Avoid overfitting to one exact gstack output format until sample artifacts are collected.
close_reason: Added gstack bridge note with positioning, import assumptions, representative input, conversion story, import report expectations, no-attack framing, manifest exposure, and focused document test.

title: Write the P0 product contract for MAP, IMPACT, and PROVE
description:
## Background
The current plan positions SEAL as systems-engineering rigor for Codex, but P0 positioning must be a precise product contract, not polished launch copy.

## Technical Approach
Draft a one-page contract that says SEAL maps repos and plans, exposes unknowns and hidden debt, analyzes proposed changes, and ties claims to evidence or gaps. Replace brittle claims with zero hidden technical debt and no untraced rework.

## Success Criteria
The repo contains a product contract that says who SEAL is for, what input it accepts, what output it produces, and why it complements or upgrades gstack-style planning.

## Test Plan
Read the copy as a non-expert and confirm it answers: what do I give it, what do I get back, and why should I trust it.

## Considerations
Keep Codex plugin language primary. ChatGPT App language should be framed as a later adapter unless implementation changes.
close_reason: Added plugin/docs/product-contract.md with audience, accepted inputs, outputs, trust model, gstack complement, and Codex-first positioning; wired it into manifest/docs/scaffold and verified with node tests\\product-contract.test.mjs, node tests\\scaffold.test.mjs, node tests\\plugin-manifest.test.mjs, npm test, bd dep cycles, and bd status.

title: Define claim and evidence taxonomy
description:
## Background
Evidence-backed rigor requires a shared model of claims, evidence, confidence, and verification status.

## Technical Approach
Define claim types such as functional, safety, reliability, security, performance, usability, launch, and operational claims. Map each to accepted evidence types.

## Success Criteria
The artifact model can represent claims and evidence in a way the validator and reports can reason about.

## Test Plan
Create example proof records for each claim type and validate them.

## Considerations
Keep the taxonomy small enough for non-expert users to understand.
close_reason: Added proof claim/evidence taxonomy, required claim type schema, taxonomy docs, generated-artifact taxonomy validation, and examples for every claim type; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Implement minimal proof evidence store
description:
## Background
A proof taxonomy without stored evidence becomes a checklist. SEAL needs the claim, evidence, and gap triad in P0 to honestly say it can prove a plan, change, or launch state.

## Technical Approach
Create .seal/proof.yaml, .seal/evidence/index.yaml, and .seal/evidence/files/. Evidence records should include id, type, claim_ids, source kind, command or source ref when relevant, captured_at, artifact_path, hash, status, and limitations.

## Success Criteria
Every proof claim can link to supporting evidence or an explicit gap, and every evidence record preserves provenance, artifact location, and hash when applicable.

## Test Plan
Record evidence from fixture test output, static inspection, external-source reference, and human approval examples, then validate claim links and file hashes.

## Considerations
Do not store sensitive command output by default. Provide a redaction or summary path where needed.
close_reason: Added minimal evidence store with provenance, redacted artifact capture, hash validation, schema/docs/fixtures, and fixture coverage; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Generate proof gap report
description:
## Background
Non-expert users need to know what is not proven and exactly what would close the gap.

## Technical Approach
Generate a human-readable report from .seal/proof.yaml and evidence records that lists unproven claims, missing evidence, stale evidence, low-confidence inference, and the next action to close each gap.

## Success Criteria
A user can read one report and understand which claims are proven, which are assumed, which are blocked, and what evidence would make them launch-ready.

## Test Plan
Run proof gap reporting on fixtures with supported claims, unsupported claims, stale evidence, and claims that intentionally remain open.

## Considerations
The report should make uncertainty visible without shaming the user for having early-stage gaps.
close_reason: Implemented proof gap report with CLI, docs, manifest wiring, and regression coverage for proven, assumed, blocked, stale, failed, and invalid claims; npm test and bd graph checks pass.

title: Configure git remote and publish initial SEAL commit
description:
## Background
AGENTS.md requires every work session to end with git pull --rebase, bd sync, git push, and a status showing the branch is up to date with origin. This repository currently has no configured remote or upstream branch.

## Technical Approach
Choose or create the authoritative remote repository, add it as origin, set the upstream for master, re-run git pull --rebase, bd sync, git push, and verify git status reports the branch is up to date with origin.

## Success Criteria
The initial SEAL commit is present on the configured remote and git status reports master is up to date with origin/master.

## Test Plan
Run git remote -v, git pull --rebase, bd sync, git push, and git status.

## Considerations
This is blocked on authority to choose the remote URL or create the remote repository.
notes: 2026-06-29: still blocked after MCP tool contract slice; git remote -v returns no remotes, so local commits cannot be pushed until a remote/upstream is configured.

title: Create release checklist and versioning process
description:
## Background
Public plugin distribution needs repeatable release discipline.

## Technical Approach
Define version bump rules, changelog expectations, packaging checks, validation commands, docs checks, and release notes.

## Success Criteria
A maintainer can cut a release by following one checklist.

## Test Plan
Dry-run the release checklist before the first public release.

## Considerations
Keep the checklist realistic for a small project.
close_reason: Added a release checklist covering version scope, local gates, manual confidence checks, bead closeout, git landing, and release notes template.

title: Add SEAL readiness levels after core gates
description:
## Background
TRL or SRL-style levels can help users understand maturity, but only after the base gates and evidence model are working.

## Technical Approach
Define lightweight readiness levels that summarize artifact completeness, repo map coverage, proof strength, and launch gate state.

## Success Criteria
Readiness levels provide a useful maturity summary without replacing concrete gate failures or proof gaps.

## Test Plan
Score fixture projects at several maturity levels and confirm the score matches the underlying gate evidence.

## Considerations
Keep readiness levels secondary to the actual evidence-backed gate report.
close_reason: Added SEAL Readiness Levels to launch reports, including SRL-0 through SRL-5 scoring tied to gate policy, map coverage, impact scope, and proof strength; documented the levels and covered pass/warn/blocked/fail maturity fixtures.

title: Create unmapped and hidden-debt register
description:
## Background
The safe promise is zero hidden technical debt, not zero technical debt. SEAL needs a visible register for gaps, orphan components, missing tests, stale artifacts, risky dependencies, and ambiguous ownership.

## Technical Approach
Write a structured register that records unmapped files, unknown owners, unlinked tests, missing evidence, stale artifacts, orphan components, risky dependencies, and ambiguous requirements with source refs and confidence.

## Success Criteria
Every unmapped or uncertain repo finding is either resolved into the map or listed as a visible debt/unknown record with a reason and next action.

## Test Plan
Run fixture repos with intentional orphan files, missing tests, stale docs, and ambiguous components and verify the register records them.

## Considerations
Debt records should be actionable and non-punitive. The goal is to turn unknowns into known unknowns.
close_reason: Added source-backed .seal/debt.yaml register, validation/reference checks, docs, and focused fixture coverage; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Define non-expert personas and guided jobs-to-be-done
description:
## Background
The plugin needs to serve planners, founders, product builders, and developers who may not know systems-engineering terminology but still need rigorous plans, traceability, and release discipline.

## Technical Approach
Write the primary personas, their inputs, their fears, their expected outputs, and the exact guided jobs SEAL should support. Include one persona that starts from gstack output and one that starts from an existing repo.

## Success Criteria
A checked-in product note names the first personas, top workflows, non-goals, and terminology that should be hidden from or explained to beginners.

## Test Plan
Walk through each persona and verify the first-run command can be described in one sentence using plain language.

## Considerations
Do not let systems-engineering vocabulary become the product surface unless paired with plain-language explanations.
close_reason: Added checked-in personas note with gstack and existing-repo personas, guided jobs, non-goals, beginner-safe terminology, and document test coverage.

title: Validate fully mapped repo coverage
description:
## Background
A project can look rigorous while major files or components remain unmapped. Coverage checks make blind spots visible.

## Technical Approach
Compare repository inventory to .seal/map.yaml and the debt register. Enforce 100 percent classification of non-ignored files, and require every non-generated, non-vendored file to map to a component or explicit unknown gap.

## Success Criteria
Coverage validation reports mapped, unmapped, ignored, generated, vendored, and unknown areas, and launch gates fail when unmapped files are not visible as gaps.

## Test Plan
Use fixture repos with known unmapped files and components.

## Considerations
Fully mapped means nothing is invisible. It does not mean SEAL magically knows every answer.
close_reason: Added file coverage validation for repository inventory versus map/debt artifacts, including unmapped, uncovered, stale, generated, vendored, unknown, and orphan component checks; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Implement cross-artifact reference validation
description:
## Background
Traceability breaks when IDs are duplicated, missing, or linked with invalid relationship types.

## Technical Approach
Validate all IDs and references across the .seal folder and report dangling refs, duplicate IDs, invalid link types, and unreachable required artifacts.

## Success Criteria
Reference problems are caught before launch reports or impact analysis trust them.

## Test Plan
Run duplicate, dangling, invalid-type, and missing-file fixtures.

## Considerations
Reference validation should run after schema validation so errors are easier to interpret.
close_reason: Wired cross-artifact reference diagnostics into seal-validate and added file-backed duplicate, dangling, invalid-link, and missing-artifact validation coverage; npm test and bd dep cycles passed.

title: Implement schema validation with actionable errors
description:
## Background
Schema validation is the first guardrail against broken artifact generation.

## Technical Approach
Wire the artifact schemas into a validator command that reports file, path, expected shape, actual value, and suggested fix where practical.

## Success Criteria
Invalid structure fails validation with messages a beginner can use.

## Test Plan
Run validator against intentionally malformed fixtures.

## Considerations
Do not expose raw validator internals as the only error message.
close_reason: Added seal-validate CLI, reusable artifact validation diagnostics, malformed fixture coverage, manifest/package/docs wiring, and passing focused, npm, and bead graph checks.
